annotations.label: Scientific notation

anchor.snippet: Scientific notation is a way of expressing numbers

annotations.note: Lean/Mathlib's `OfScientific.ofScientific` literal machinery (m × 10^±e) implements scientific notation, but there is no general mathematical definition of the concept.

annotations.label: Significand and exponent

anchor.snippet: The integer n is called the exponent and the real number m is called the significand

annotations.note: `OfScientific.ofScientific` takes a mantissa `m`, sign `s`, and exponent `e`, but the significand/exponent terminology is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Decimal floating point

anchor.snippet: Decimal floating point is a computer arithmetic system

annotations.note: Mathlib does not model decimal floating-point computer arithmetic systems.

annotations.label: Multiple representations of a real number

anchor.snippet: Any real number can be written in the form

annotations.note: `Int.log` plus its bounds `Int.zpow_log_le_self`/`Int.lt_zpow_succ_log_self` provide the exponent placing any positive real in [b^n, b^(n+1)), but no explicit `r = m·10^n` decomposition lemma is stated.

annotations.label: Normalized scientific notation

anchor.snippet: the exponent n is chosen so that the absolute value of m remains at least one and less than ten

annotations.note: `Int.log b r` is exactly the normalizing exponent, with `Int.zpow_log_le_self`/`Int.lt_zpow_succ_log_self` giving b^log ≤ r < b^(log+1) (i.e. 1 ≤ r/b^log < b), but the normalized-form definition itself is not packaged.

annotations.label: Negative exponent for numbers between 0 and 1

anchor.snippet: the exponent n is negative for a number with absolute value between 0 and 1

annotations.note: `Int.log_of_right_le_one` shows `log b r = -Nat.clog b ⌈r⁻¹⌉₊ ≤ 0` for r ≤ 1, matching the negative-exponent claim, though stated about `Int.log` rather than scientific notation.

annotations.label: Exponential notation

anchor.snippet: Normalized scientific notation is often called exponential notation

annotations.note: This is merely an alternative name and carries no formalizable mathematical content.

annotations.label: Engineering notation

anchor.snippet: differs from normalized scientific notation in that the exponent n is restricted to multiples of 3

annotations.note: The engineering-notation convention (exponent a multiple of 3) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Engineering notation (nanometres)

anchor.snippet: twelve-point-five nanometres

annotations.note: A units example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: E notation

anchor.snippet: for a decimal significand m and integer exponent n means the same as

annotations.note: Lean's `OfScientific.ofScientific m s e` literal syntax realizes E-notation (`mEn = m·10^n`), but the notation convention is not stated as a definition.

annotations.label: E notation example

anchor.snippet: is written as 6.022E23 or 6.022e23

annotations.note: A specific numeric notation example with no formalized statement.

annotations.label: Significant figure

anchor.snippet: A significant figure is a digit in a number that adds to its precision

annotations.note: The notion of significant figures (measurement precision) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conversion scales to between 1 and 10

anchor.snippet: When a number is converted into normalized scientific notation, it is scaled down to a number between 1 and 10

annotations.note: The scaling into [1,10) is the same normalization captured by `Int.zpow_log_le_self`/`Int.lt_zpow_succ_log_self`, but no conversion procedure is formalized.

annotations.label: Recording an estimated final digit

anchor.snippet: It is customary in scientific measurement to record all the definitely known digits

annotations.note: A measurement-recording convention, not a mathematical statement.

anchor.snippet: the accepted value of the mass of the proton can be expressed as

annotations.note: A physical-constant example with no Mathlib counterpart.

anchor.snippet: The Earth's circumference is approximately

annotations.label: Inch in scientific notation

anchor.snippet: An inch is defined as exactly

annotations.note: A units-definition example, not formalized.

annotations.label: Converting a number

anchor.snippet: Converting a number in these cases means to either convert the number into scientific notation form

annotations.note: An informal description of conversion, not formalized.

annotations.label: Decimal to scientific procedure

anchor.snippet: First, move the decimal separator point sufficient places

annotations.note: A digit-shifting procedure, not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Scientific to decimal procedure

anchor.snippet: Converting a number from scientific notation to decimal notation

annotations.note: An informal conversion procedure, not formalized.

annotations.label: Exponential conversion procedure

anchor.snippet: Conversion between different scientific notation representations of the same number with different exponential values

annotations.note: Re-expressing m·10^n with a different exponent is an informal procedure, not a formalized statement.

annotations.label: Setup of two numbers in scientific notation

anchor.snippet: Given two numbers in scientific notation

annotations.note: A setup for arithmetic operations, not a formalizable statement.

anchors.snippet: Given two numbers in scientific notation

annotations.label: Multiplication and division rule

anchor.snippet: Multiplication and division are performed using the rules for operation with exponentiation

annotations.note: The exponent law `zpow_add₀` (a^(m+n)=a^m·a^n) underlies multiplying (10^n₁)(10^n₂)=10^(n₁+n₂), but the scientific-notation product rule is not stated directly.

anchors.snippet: Multiplication and division are performed using the rules for operation with exponentiation

annotations.label: Addition and subtraction rule

anchor.snippet: Addition and subtraction require the numbers to be represented using the same exponential part

annotations.note: Aligning exponents then factoring via distributivity is an informal procedure, not formalized as a scientific-notation rule.

annotations.label: Other bases for scientific notation

anchor.snippet: While base ten is normally used for scientific notation, powers of other bases can be used too

annotations.note: A meta-remark that any base may be used; `Int.log` is base-parameterized but no scientific-notation statement is formalized.

annotations.label: Base-2 scientific notation

anchor.snippet: in base-2 scientific notation, the number 1001 b in binary

annotations.note: A binary notation example, not formalized.

annotations.label: B notation for base-2 exponent

anchor.snippet: a base-2 exponent is sometimes also indicated by using the letter "B" instead of "E"

annotations.note: A typographic notation convention, not formalized.

annotations.label: H and O notation for base-16 and base-8 exponents

anchor.snippet: are sometimes also used to indicate times 16 or 8 to the power

annotations.note: Typographic notation conventions, not formalized.

annotations.label: P notation

anchor.snippet: Another similar convention to denote base-2 exponents is using a letter "P"

annotations.note: A typographic notation convention (hex-float 'p'), not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Engineering notation as base-1000

anchor.snippet: Engineering notation can be viewed as a base-1000 scientific notation

annotations.note: Reinterpreting engineering notation as base-1000 is not formalized.
